episode_meta.title: Building AI Products with an Editor-Centric Approach

ai_tools: LLM-powered headline and lead suggestion system
prototyping platform for Gen AI tasks
synthetic voices for AI-powered video explainers

use_cases.description: Prototyping platform for Gen AI tasks allowing editors to transcribe interviews, summarize articles, rephrase articles in plain language, and generate video scripts augmented with synthetic voices
use_cases.outcome: Editors can perform a wide range of generative AI tasks from a single platform

use_cases.description: LLM-powered system integrated into CMS that gives editors suggestions for headlines and leads
use_cases.outcome: Editors receive AI-generated headline and lead suggestions directly in CMS

use_cases.description: Tool to suggest how to illustrate a news story using text-to-image models, generating only illustration-style pictures (not photorealistic) to avoid confusion with real photos
use_cases.outcome: Images are instantly recognizable as AI-generated; reduces time-consuming illustration task

use_cases.description: Self-evaluation system for LLM output that calculates metrics (correctness, completeness, comprehensiveness) against a threshold, reprompting automatically if threshold is not met
use_cases.outcome: Reduces hallucination risk; only content meeting quality threshold is presented to editors for final review

use_cases.description: Fine-tuning task-specific large language models on IPPEN's own articles using open-source models
use_cases.outcome: Task-specific models trained on proprietary content

use_cases.description: AI feature in apps allowing readers to read articles in different formats: bullet points, longer summaries, translated into another language, or in plain/simple language
use_cases.outcome: Readers can choose format that fits their current reading situation

use_cases.description: Editorial assistant tool co-developed with intern editors to catch editorial, stylistic, and grammatical errors, addressing quality risks especially during weekends and evenings
use_cases.outcome: Tested with four different newsrooms/departments; addresses style guide compliance under time pressure

use_cases.description: Transcription tool that evolved to include translation (e.g., English to German), summarization, bullet point extraction, and identification of strongest quotes from audio
use_cases.outcome: Features developed iteratively based on direct editor feedback

use_cases.description: Early experimentation editing newswire content using AI
use_cases.outcome: Foundation for later AI tool development

use_cases.description: Microsoft replaced human journalists curating MSN website and Edge browser with an AI-powered curation system
use_cases.outcome: Journalists were let go; negative consequences for people involved and quality of news coverage

key_claims.claim: IPPEN started AI workshops for editors on November 29, 2022 -- exactly one day before the launch of ChatGPT -- demonstrating they were already investing in AI education before the generative AI hype
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Replacing human journalists with AI systems at Microsoft News had negative consequences both for the people directly involved and for the quality of news coverage
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: The risk of editorial, stylistic, and grammatical errors is becoming bigger especially at weekends and in the evening due to time pressure
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Editor-centric product development where editors are involved at all stages leads to tools that bring real business value and match editor workflows
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Text-to-image models should only produce illustration-style pictures that are instantly recognizable as AI-generated, never images that could be mistaken for photos
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: When something goes wrong with AI-generated content, it is not the algorithm's fault -- the newsroom is accountable
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: The LLM self-evaluation system can reprompt itself based on editor-derived feedback to address errors and reduce hallucination risk
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

challenges.description: Risk of journalists being replaced by AI systems, as experienced firsthand at Microsoft News where contracts were cancelled
Maintaining trust in news media in the generative AI era requires strict guardrails, human-in-the-loop, and transparency
LLM hallucination risk requiring a dedicated self-evaluation system to check factuality and completeness of AI-generated content
Ensuring AI-generated images are not mistaken for real photos, requiring deliberate restriction to illustration-style outputs
Maintaining editorial quality (style guide compliance, grammatical accuracy) under time pressure, especially during weekends and evenings
Countering unrealistic hype around LLMs (e.g., 'five prompts that will make you more productive') by helping editors develop a more realistic understanding of AI capabilities and limitations
Ensuring transparency both internally (educating editors) and externally (bylines disclosing AI use to readers)
Prioritizing which AI product ideas to pursue from many possibilities using scalability, impact-effort metrics, and feasibility criteria

quotes.text: I've been in a situation where I had to tell my colleagues, I'm sorry, we have to cancel your contract. And I've seen what happens when you replace human journalists with AI systems and have seen which kind of consequences this can have... for the people who are directly involved but also for the quality of the news coverage.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Consequences of replacing journalists with AI

quotes.text: When we talk about responsible use, I talk about the fact that if something goes wrong and it can happen, unfortunately, then it's not the algorithm's fault that something has been published, that it was false, but it's our own. We are accountable for that.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

quotes.text: We do not create images that could be mistaken for photos. So we only produce illustration style pictures because they are instantly recognizable as AI generated pictures.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Responsible use of text-to-image AI in newsrooms

quotes.text: We usually don't ask them, what do you want to have? Because this will be a bit tricky question, but we try to better understand how they work and later analyze their feedback so we have a better idea of their work day and their pain points.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Product discovery methodology for AI tools

quotes.text: They are not just users. They are co-creators.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

quotes.text: I think it's our responsibility to help editors to develop a more realistic approach to LLMs.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Countering AI hype with realistic expectations

recommendations.advice: Follow an editor-centric product development process where editors are involved at all stages -- as co-creators, not just end users
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom product managers and AI teams

recommendations.advice: Establish clear guardrails and principles (human-in-the-loop, responsible AI use, transparency) before developing AI tools
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Create dedicated internship programs where editors from different departments spend two weeks embedded with the AI team to co-develop and test tools
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom leaders and AI product teams

recommendations.advice: Build cross-functional AI teams that include editors alongside machine learning experts, NLP experts, and developers
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Conduct product discovery sessions with editors through deep-dive interviews focused on understanding workflows and pain points rather than asking 'what do you want'
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Only use AI-generated images in illustration style that are instantly recognizable as non-photographic to maintain trust
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Implement LLM self-evaluation systems with metrics like correctness and completeness to reduce hallucination risk before content reaches editors
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
recommendations.target_audience: AI engineers and product teams in media

recommendations.advice: Prioritize AI tools using standardization (scalability across newsrooms), impact-effort metrics, feasibility, and speed of development, aligned with overall company strategy via OKRs
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Use bylines at the bottom of articles to make the use of generative AI transparent for readers
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

external_references: Journalism AI Fellowship Programme at the London School of Economics
Aleph Alpha (German company developing its own LLMs)
The Guardian opinion piece: 'A Robot Wrote This Entire Article. Are You Scared Yet, Human?' (GPT-3 generated)
Ariel Zirulnick (News Product Alliance mentor, impact-effort metrics framework)
